1.synthesis: The final chapter assigns each tribe its lateral strip of land, organized from north to south. The northern strips are: Dan, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Reuben, and Judah — each receiving one strip running from the eastern border to the western border, from the great sea (vv.1–7). Adjacent to Judah’s territory on the south is the sacred district: the priestly zone (twenty-five thousand by ten thousand cubits), with the sanctuary in its center; the Levitical zone of equal size; and the city zone of five thousand by twenty-five thousand (vv.8–14). The Zadokite priests who drew near to serve YHWH receive the most holy portion adjacent to the sanctuary, with the altar in its midst (vv.11–12). The Levites receive an equal strip adjacent to the Zadokite territory (vv.13–14). The tribal arrangement with Judah immediately north of the sacred district and Benjamin immediately south places the two tribes historically associated with Jerusalem and the Davidic monarchy as the closest to the divine dwelling. Dan, exiled by geography and history to the extremity, is nonetheless given the northernmost strip — excluded from nothing, present on every side.
1.historical_context: The arrangement of seven tribes north and five tribes south of the central sacred district differs significantly from the historical allotments of Joshua. The Transjordan tribes (Reuben, Gad, East Manasseh) are incorporated west of the Jordan in the new arrangement — the land is unified without the river-division that historically separated the two and a half eastern tribes. Judah’s position immediately north of the sacred district evokes the Davidic connection: Judah is the royal tribe, closest to the divine dwelling. Dan’s position at the far north, despite its history of idolatry at Dan (1 Kings 12:29), reflects the eschatological inclusion of all twelve without remainder.
1.literary_structure: The chapter unfolds with military precision: seven northern tribes, each specified in a single verse; the sacred district (detailed in vv.8-22 but summarized here as the Zadokite-Levitical-city arrangement); five southern tribes. This enumeration creates a comprehensive accounting that leaves no tribe unplaced. The systematic listing before and after the sacred center gives the vision its territorial completeness — every tribe has its place in the new order.
1.theological_application: The placement of all twelve tribes, including Dan (which had abandoned Yahwistic purity) and Reuben (first-born who lost his inheritance for violation), suggests that the eschatological order is one of restoration rather than meritocratic sorting. No tribe is excluded; every tribe has its strip. The sacred district at the center does not elevate one tribe over another in territorial terms — each strip is the same width. The equality of tribal strips models a community where the sacred center organizes all without privileging any lineage for mere hereditary reasons.
1.christology: The inclusion of all twelve tribes in the eschatological land is the territorial counterpart to the twelve apostles of the Lamb on the twelve foundation stones of the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:14). The eschatological community is organized around a perfect twelve — the fullness of the covenant people restored and extended. Christ as the one from the tribe of Judah (whose territory borders the sacred district directly) is the mediating king through whom all twelve tribes find their place. Revelation 7:4–8 seals twelve thousand from every tribe — an eschatological census that picks up the comprehensive inclusion of Ezekiel’s tribal strips.

15.synthesis: From the twenty-five thousand cubit width of the sacred district, a strip of five thousand cubits is for common use: for the city, for dwellings, and for pasturelands (v.15). The city is forty-five hundred cubits square on its four sides (v.16). The pastureland for the city: two hundred and fifty cubits on each of its four sides (v.17). The remainder of the length along the sacred portion is ten thousand cubits on the east side and ten thousand on the west side — for food for the workers of the city (v.18). The workers of the city from all the tribes of Israel shall cultivate it (v.19). The entire district shall be twenty-five thousand by twenty-five thousand cubits — a perfect square set apart as holy, with the city’s portion included (v.20). The remainder on both sides of the sacred district and the city allotment belongs to the prince — running from twenty-five thousand cubits on the east to the eastern border, and from twenty-five thousand on the west to the western border (v.21). The city is governed by and sustained from the agricultural land worked by all the tribes together — a civic economy without tribal monopoly.
15.historical_context: The city of forty-five hundred cubits square (roughly 2.4 km per side using the long cubit) is smaller than Herod’s Jerusalem but proportionally large for the ancient world. The twelve gates named for the tribes (vv.30–35) place all of Israel’s identity on the city walls. The agricultural land surrounding the city, cultivated by workers from all the tribes together, models a non-tribal economic partnership at the city level. The prince’s land flanking the central district on both sides (east and west) means the civic center is held between royal territories without the royal residence being within the sacred zone — the lesson of 43:8 applied in land terms.
15.literary_structure: The city allotment is precisely nested within the larger twenty-five thousand cubit district: five thousand cubit city-strip within the twenty-five thousand; city forty-five hundred square within the five thousand strip; pastureland two hundred fifty on each side of the city. The nested geometry continues the concentric logic of the entire vision. The total twenty-five thousand by twenty-five thousand sacred district that includes the city reinforces the master square that has governed every dimension of the vision since ch40.
15.theological_application: The agricultural land cultivated jointly by all tribes for the city’s food supply models a communal economic base for the civic center. The city’s workers come from all tribes — it is not a tribal city but a pan-Israelite city. This prefigures the New Jerusalem as a city that belongs to all the people of God, not to any one lineage or tradition. The city is fed by the land; the land is cultivated by all; the harvest sustains the community of worship centered on the sanctuary. Every part serves every other part.
15.christology: The city with pastureland and agricultural zone surrounding it, fed by workers from all tribes, anticipates the New Jerusalem’s fully sustained community. Revelation 21:24–26 describes the nations bringing their glory into the city — a tribute of creative flourishing from all peoples to the city of God. The civic economy of Ezekiel’s vision (multi-tribal agriculture sustaining the city of the sanctuary) points to the new creation’s fully integrated provision, where nothing is lacking and all contribute to the whole under the governance of the Lamb.

23.synthesis: South of the sacred district, five more tribal strips are allotted: Benjamin, Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun, and Gad — each receiving one horizontal strip from east border to west border, in that order from north to south (vv.23–28). The declaration follows: this is the land you shall apportion as an inheritance among the tribes of Israel, and these are their portions (v.29). Benjamin’s position immediately south of the sacred district mirrors Judah’s position immediately north — the two tribes of the historical southern kingdom bracket the divine dwelling on both sides. Benjamin was the tribe of the original sanctuary city Mizpah, the tribe of Saul, and the tribe within whose territory Jerusalem originally fell (Joshua 18:28). Gad’s position at the southern extreme, like Dan at the northern extreme, places a tribe historically at the edges of Israelite territory and loyalty at the boundaries of the new land — included, restored, and allotted.
23.historical_context: The five southern tribes complete the twelve-tribe enumeration across both northern and southern strips. Simeon’s inclusion is notable: Simeon’s tribal inheritance had effectively been absorbed into Judah by the time of the monarchy (Joshua 19:1 — Simeon’s allotment was within Judah’s), and Simeon as a distinct tribal entity largely disappears from historical record. In the new land, Simeon receives its own distinct strip — a restoration of tribal identity that history had erased. Similarly, Gad (a Transjordan tribe by historical allotment) receives land in the unified territory west of the Jordan. The new order restores and reconstitutes what time and political history had dissolved.
23.literary_structure: The five-verse enumeration of five tribes mirrors the seven-verse enumeration of seven northern tribes, completing the symmetrical survey. The closing declaration of v.29 — “this is the land you shall apportion as an inheritance... these are their portions” — functions as the formal conclusion of the land division that began in 47:13. The formal conclusion signals that what follows — the city gates and the city’s name — is the climactic finale, not continuation of the allotment discussion.
23.theological_application: The restoration of Simeon as a distinct tribe — reconstituted from historical absorption — models the eschatological principle that what has been lost or dissolved in the processes of time and history is not permanently absent from YHWH’s plan. Tribes that disappeared, communities that scattered, identities that dissolved are all present in the final accounting. This is the territorial expression of the dry bones vision (Ezekiel 37:1–14): what appeared permanently dead is reconstituted by YHWH’s word and breath. The new order does not work only with what survived — it restores what perished.
23.christology: Paul’s declaration that in Christ “there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female” (Galatians 3:28) addresses the dissolution of the identity-markers that structured the old order — but it does not erase the persons who bore those identities. The new community in Christ incorporates every tribe, every lineage, every scattered people into a unity that does not abolish particularity but fulfills it. Simeon restored in the eschatological allotment is the type of every community that seemed irretrievably lost and is found again in the new creation.

30.pericope_label: The Gates of the New City
30.synthesis: The city has exits on four sides. On the north, measuring forty-five hundred cubits, are three gates: the gate of Reuben, the gate of Judah, the gate of Levi (v.31). On the east, forty-five hundred cubits, three gates: the gate of Joseph, the gate of Benjamin, the gate of Dan (v.32). On the south, forty-five hundred cubits, three gates: the gate of Simeon, the gate of Issachar, the gate of Zebulun (v.33). On the west, forty-five hundred cubits, three gates: the gate of Gad, the gate of Asher, the gate of Naphtali (v.34). The circumference of the city is eighteen thousand cubits. And the name of the city from that time on shall be: YHWH Shammah — “The LORD Is There” (v.35). Twelve gates for twelve tribes on four sides — three per side — for a city whose name is not “Temple” or “Holy” or even “Jerusalem,” but the unadorned declaration of divine presence: YHWH is there. The entire book of Ezekiel — the departure of the glory, the judgment of the city, the restoration, the measurements, the return of the glory — leads to this single sentence.
30.historical_context: The twelve-gate arrangement, three per side, is the structural template that Revelation 21:12–13 adopts directly for the New Jerusalem: “twelve gates, and at the gates twelve angels, and on the gates the names of the twelve tribes of the sons of Israel were inscribed — on the east three gates, on the north three gates, on the south three gates, and on the west three gates.” Levi’s inclusion in the gate list (v.31) — replacing the standard Ephraim-Manasseh double-counting — gives Levi its own gate on the city rather than a territory in the land, consistent with the Levites’ sanctuary portion rather than tribal strip. The name YHWH Shammah contrasts with Jerusalem’s common designation and with the “YHWH-Tsidkenu” (YHWH our Righteousness) of Jeremiah 23:6.
30.literary_structure: The book of Ezekiel begins with “I saw visions of God” (1:1) and ends with “The LORD Is There.” The trajectory from visionary encounter to residential permanence is the arc of the entire book. The four-sided gate enumeration creates a complete circle around the city; the eighteen-thousand-cubit circumference closes the spatial frame opened by the initial measurement of 40:5. The city’s name is the book’s final word — and it is a declaration, not a description.
30.theological_application: YHWH Shammah is the answer to the book’s central problem: YHWH left. The departure of the glory in ch.10–11 was the greatest catastrophe of the book — greater than the physical destruction of the city, because the city’s value lay in its being the dwelling place of the holy God. The return of the glory in ch.43 and the naming of the city “YHWH Is There” is the resolution: permanent divine presence is the eschatological goal. Not a magnificent building, not a perfect social order, not a prosperous land — but simply: YHWH is there. Everything else in the vision is the architectural and social expression of that single fact.
30.christology: YHWH Shammah — The LORD Is There — is realized in the incarnation of the Son: “Emmanuel, which means God with us” (Matthew 1:23). The name that crowns Ezekiel’s vision is the name that defines Jesus’ identity. His promise “I am with you always, to the end of the age” (Matthew 28:20) is the new-covenant YHWH Shammah. The New Jerusalem of Revelation 21:3 declares: “Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them.” And Revelation 22:3: “the throne of God and of the Lamb will be in it.” The city is named by the presence of God; the presence of God is the person of Jesus Christ; the city whose name is YHWH Shammah is the eternal dwelling of those united to him.
